info.name: joe-404/laravel-auth
info.description:
Complete API collection for the joe-404/laravel-auth Laravel library.

**Two ready-to-use folders:**
- **Web & SPA — Session / Cookie** — for browser/SPA clients. Uses Laravel session cookies + CSRF token. No Authorization header needed on authenticated requests. A folder-level pre-request script automatically fetches `/sanctum/csrf-cookie` before every request in this folder.
- **Mobile — Bearer Token** — for mobile apps and API clients. Uses `Authorization: Bearer {{auth_token}}` on every authenticated request. Sends `X-Client-Type: mobile` on login to get a token + refresh token.

**Quick start (Web/SPA mode):**
1. Set `base_url` variable to your app URL
2. Run **Setup → Get CSRF Cookie** once
3. Call **Web & SPA / Registration / 1. Initiate Registration** (email only — no password)
4. Verify email via **2a. Verify OTP** or **2b. Verify Magic Link** — this saves a `completion_token`
5. Call **3. Complete Registration** with the password to finalize the account
6. Then **Login** — session cookie is stored automatically by Postman's cookie jar

**Quick start (Mobile mode):**
1. Set `base_url` variable
2. Call **Mobile / Registration / 1. Initiate Registration** (email only)
3. Verify email — saves `completion_token`
4. Call **3. Complete Registration** — `auth_token` and `refresh_token` are saved automatically
5. Use `POST /auth/token/refresh` before token expires

**Three-step registration:**
The library now uses a 3-step registration flow. The user's password is NEVER sent to `POST /auth/register`. Instead:
1. `POST /auth/register` with `{email}` only → returns `temp_token`
2. Verify email (OTP or magic link) → returns `completion_token` (UUID, 15 min TTL)
3. `POST /auth/register/complete` with `{completion_token, password, password_confirmation}` → creates the user, fires `EmailVerified`, logs them in

**Variables:**
- `base_url` — your Laravel app URL (e.g. `http://localhost:8000`)
- `auth_token` — auto-saved on login / register-complete (mobile) / Google OAuth
- `refresh_token` — auto-saved on login / register-complete (mobile) / token refresh
- `xsrf_token` — auto-saved by SPA folder pre-request script
- `temp_token` — auto-saved after registration initiation
- `completion_token` — auto-saved after email verification (step 2); consumed in step 3
- `reset_token` — auto-saved after magic link password reset step
- `api_token_id` — auto-saved after creating an API token
- `api_token_raw` — auto-saved after creating an API token
- `session_id` — auto-saved after listing sessions
- `locale` — value of the `Accept-Language` header (v2.3+). Default `en`. Change to `ar`, `fr`, etc. for localized messages.

**v2.2 customization (opt-in, no endpoint changes):** `auth_system.referral_code.*` generates referral codes on register-complete; `auth_system.messages.*` overrides individual response strings; `auth_system.registration.extra_fields_messages` overrides validation wording; `auth_system.registration.extra_fields_transformers` derives fields post-validation.

**v2.4 account lifecycle (new endpoints):** `DELETE /auth/account` self-service deletes the user with a 30-day grace window. Logging in again during grace silently restores the account — no separate restore endpoint. After grace, the hourly `PurgeExpiredAccountDeletions` job nulls every unique column on the users row so the email/username can be reclaimed.

**v2.4 admin status + timed bans:** `GET|POST /auth/admin/users/{id}/status` reads and changes a user's `account_status` (`active`/`disabled`/`suspended`). The update endpoint accepts an optional `expires_at` (ISO 8601) OR `duration_minutes` for **timed bans** — when the moment passes the package auto-flips the user back to `active` via a lazy revert on the next login and a scheduled `RevertExpiredAccountStatuses` sweep (every 5 min by default). The `account.status.auto_unban.temporary_statuses` config whitelists which statuses can carry an expiry (default `['suspended']`); other statuses are permanent-only and reject expiry inputs with 422. Null expiry on a banned account = permanent ban.

See the **Account (v2.4)** subfolder under each of Web/SPA + Mobile, and the **Admin — Account Status (v2.4)** folder at the bottom. Full docs at `docs/account-status.md` and `docs/account-deletion.md`.

**v2.3 multi-language (opt-in, no endpoint changes):** Publish translations via `php artisan vendor:publish --tag=auth-lang`, then translate `lang/vendor/auth_system/<locale>/{messages,errors}.php`. The package reads `app()->getLocale()` at response time, so the `Accept-Language` header on each request (or your own locale middleware) localizes every success and error message. See `docs/localization.md`.

variable.description: v2.3+ — sent as the Accept-Language header so the package can localize success and error messages. Default 'en'. Set to any locale you've translated (e.g. 'ar', 'fr').
v2.6 — auto-saved on Login when 2FA is enrolled. Consumed by POST /auth/2fa/challenge.
v2.6 — one-time device token, auto-saved by POST /auth/2fa/challenge when trust_device=true. Send it back as the X-Trusted-Device-Token header on future logins to bypass 2FA.
v2.6 — auto-saved from the OAuth callback when social.profile_completion is enabled. Consumed by POST /auth/social/complete.

item.name: Setup
item.description: Run these once before using the Web & SPA folder.

request.description:
**Run this before making any Web/SPA mode requests.**

Sanctum sets two cookies:
- `laravel_session` — your session identifier
- `XSRF-TOKEN` — the CSRF token

The test script saves the token to `xsrf_token`. The Web & SPA folder's pre-request script also does this automatically before every request.

**Not required for the Mobile / Bearer Token folder.**

item.name: Web & SPA — Session / Cookie
item.description:
All requests configured for browser/SPA clients.

**How it works:**
- A folder-level pre-request script fetches `/sanctum/csrf-cookie` automatically before every request and saves the token to `xsrf_token`.
- All mutating requests (POST/DELETE/PATCH) include `X-XSRF-TOKEN: {{xsrf_token}}`.
- Authenticated requests use the session cookie set by Postman's cookie jar — no Authorization header needed.
- Login and Complete Registration return `token: null`; Sanctum sets the session cookie on the response.

**Required:** `AUTH_MODE=web` or `AUTH_MODE=both` (without `AUTH_SPA_TOKEN=true`).

exec: pm.sendRequest({ url: baseUrl + '/sanctum/csrf-cookie', method: 'GET' }, function(err, res) {

request.description:
**Step 1 of 3.** Initiates registration. Body is `{email}` only — **NO password** is sent at this step. Sends OTP + magic link to the email (one combined email when `AUTH_VERIFICATION_METHOD=both`).

Does **not** create the user yet — that happens in step 3 after the password is set.

Auto-saves `temp_token` for Reverb channel subscription. After verifying the email (step 2) you will receive a `completion_token` to use in step 3.

**Extra fields example** — if your app collects more data at signup, add them here (still no password):
```json
{
  "email": "joe@example.com",
  "phone": "+96170000000",
  "birthday": "1990-01-01"
}
```

**Errors:** 409 already registered · 422 validation · 429 rate limit

response.name: 201 — Verification email sent
409 — Email already registered
422 — Validation error

request.description:
**Step 2 of 3.** Returns a `completion_token` (UUID, 15 min TTL). Use it in step 3 to set your password and finalize the account.

Replace `123456` with the actual OTP from the email.

**Errors:** 422 OTP invalid or expired

response.name: 201 — Email verified, completion_token issued
422 — Invalid OTP

request.description:
**Step 2 of 3 (alternative).** Returns a `completion_token` (UUID, 15 min TTL). Use it in step 3 to set your password and finalize the account.

Paste the full magic link URL from the verification email. The library validates the signature.

When `AUTH_MAGIC_LINK_TARGET=frontend`, your frontend receives `?token=uuid` and calls this endpoint with the token.

**Errors:** 422 invalid/expired signature or token already used

response.name: 201 — Email verified, completion_token issued

exec: console.log('SPA mode — session cookie set, no token returned.');

request.description:
**Step 3 of 3.** Finalizes registration by setting the user's password. The `completion_token` is consumed (one-time use). On success the user is created, assigned the default role, logged in, and the `EmailVerified` event fires.

In SPA mode the response `token` is `null` — Sanctum sets the session cookie.

**Errors:** 422 completion_token invalid/expired · 422 password validation

response.name: 201 — Registration complete (SPA)
422 — completion_token invalid
422 — Password validation

request.description: Resends the verification OTP/link for a pending (unverified) registration. Always returns 200 to prevent email enumeration.

response.name: 200 — Sent (or pretended)

exec: console.log('SPA mode — session cookie set by Sanctum. No token returned.');

request.description:
Logs in and receives a session cookie. The response `token` is `null` — Sanctum sets `laravel_session` and `XSRF-TOKEN` cookies that Postman stores automatically.

**Important:** This request intentionally has no `Accept: application/json` header. Sending that header can trigger token mode in `AUTH_MODE=both`. Remove it so the server sees a browser-like request.

**Errors:** 401 wrong credentials/locked · 403 inactive/unverified · 422 validation · 429 rate limit

response.name: 423 — Account locked

request.description: Returns the authenticated user's profile, roles, permissions, and active session count. Authenticated via session cookie — no Authorization header needed.
Revokes the current session only. The session cookie is invalidated.
Revokes ALL sessions and tokens for this user across every device.
Sends OTP and/or magic link for password reset. Always returns 200 to prevent email enumeration.

response.name: 200 — Reset link sent (or pretended)

request.description:
Step 1 of 2 for OTP-based password reset. Validates the OTP sent to the user's email and returns a short-lived reset_token (15 min). Use the reset_token in **2c. Confirm Reset** to set the new password.

**Errors:** 422 OTP invalid, 422 OTP expired, 422 validation

response.name: 200 — OTP verified, reset_token issued
422 — OTP expired
422 — Validation error (missing field)

request.description:
Paste the full URL from the password reset email. Validates the signature and returns a `reset_token` valid for 15 minutes.

Auto-saves `reset_token` to collection variables. Then call **2c. Confirm Reset**.

response.name: 200 — Magic link valid

exec: console.log('Password reset complete. Session cookie set by server.');

request.description:
Final step of password reset. Accepts the reset_token from Step 2a or 2b and sets a new password.

- **logout_all: true** (default) — revoke all existing sessions/tokens before logging in
- **logout_all: false** — keep existing sessions, just update the password and log in

The user is automatically logged in after a successful reset. Response mirrors the login response: Bearer token for mobile/API clients, session cookie for web/SPA clients.

**Errors:** 422 reset_token invalid/expired, 422 validation

response.name: 200 — Reset + auto-login (logout_all: true)
200 — Reset + auto-login (logout_all: false, keep sessions)
422 — reset_token invalid or expired
422 — Validation error

request.description:
Changes password for the currently authenticated user.

**`logout_all: true`** — revokes all other sessions except the current one.

**Errors:** 422 current_password incorrect or validation failed

response.name: 422 — Current password incorrect

request.description:
Returns ALL active sessions for the authenticated user — the current session and every other logged-in device — sorted by most recently active. Each entry includes platform, browser, OS, device info, IP, geo location, and an is_current flag marking which session belongs to this request.

The test script auto-saves a non-current session_id for use in the Revoke request below.

**Errors:** 401 unauthenticated

response.name: 200 — All sessions (current + others)
response.body:
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Sessions retrieved.",
  "data": {
    "sessions": [
      {
        "id": 3,
        "platform": "mobile",
        "browser": null,
        "os": "Android",
        "device_model": "SM-G991B",
        "device_marketing_name": "Samsung Galaxy S21",
        "device_code": "samsung_sm-g991b",
        "device_platform": "android",
        "ip_address": "10.0.0.5",
        "city": "Beirut",
        "country": "LB",
        "last_active_at": "2026-05-15T14:00:00.000000Z",
        "created_at": "2026-05-15T09:00:00.000000Z",
        "is_current": true
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "platform": "web",
        "browser": "Firefox",
        "os": "macOS",
        "device_model": null,
        "device_marketing_name": null,
        "device_code": null,
        "device_platform": null,
        "ip_address": "192.168.1.20",
        "city": "Paris",
        "country": "FR",
        "last_active_at": "2026-05-14T18:30:00.000000Z",
        "created_at": "2026-05-14T10:00:00.000000Z",
        "is_current": false
      },
      {
        "id": 1,
        "platform": "web",
        "browser": "Chrome",
        "os": "Windows",
        "device_model": null,
        "device_marketing_name": null,
        "device_code": null,
        "device_platform": null,
        "ip_address": "192.168.1.10",
        "city": "Beirut",
        "country": "LB",
        "last_active_at": "2026-05-13T08:00:00.000000Z",
        "created_at": "2026-05-13T08:00:00.000000Z",
        "is_current": false
      }
    ]
  }
}

request.description: Revokes the session with the given ID. Also revokes the associated Sanctum token if it was a token-based session.

Lists all third-party API tokens owned by the authenticated user.

**Requires:** `AUTH_MODE=api` or `AUTH_MODE=both`.

Creates a new third-party API token. The `raw_token` in the response is shown **only once** — store it securely.

**`abilities`:** array of strings. Use `["*"]` for full access.
**`expires_in_days`:** `null` = never expires. Max 3650.

Token format: `auth_at_{base64}`

Auto-saves `api_token_raw` and `api_token_id`.

response.name: 201 — Token created

request.description: Revokes the API token with the given ID. Uses `api_token_id` saved by the Create request.

Returns the Google OAuth authorization URL. Copy `data.redirect_url` and open it in a browser to start the OAuth flow.

**Requires:** `AUTH_GOOGLE_ENABLED=true`

**Errors:** 403 Google OAuth disabled

Google redirects the user's browser here after authorization. In practice you do not call this directly — Google's redirect handles it.

**Handles 3 cases automatically:** existing provider link · email match · new user.

**Errors:** 403 disabled or inactive · 400 OAuth exchange failed

response.name: 202 — Requires link confirmation

item.name: Account (v2.4)
item.description:
Self-service lifecycle endpoints. Two distinct flows:

**Delete (heavy, with grace period)** — `DELETE /auth/account` soft-deletes the user, snapshots the row into `deleted_accounts`, schedules a 30-day purge. Logging in during grace silently restores. After grace, the worker nulls unique columns so the email can be reclaimed.

**Deactivate (Instagram-style pause)** — `POST /auth/account/deactivate` flips the user to `deactivated` and revokes every session. No countdown, no anonymisation. The user can come back any time by logging in normally — the login flow auto-reactivates silently.

**Pick based on intent:**
- User wants to leave but might change their mind → **Delete** (30-day window)
- User wants to take a break and come back → **Deactivate** (no deadline)

request.description:
Soft-deletes the authenticated user. Body:

- `password` (required when `account.deletion.require_password=true`, default true)
- `reason` (optional free text, stored in `deleted_accounts.delete_reason`)

**Side effects:**
- snapshots the user row into `deleted_accounts`
- sets `account_status = deleted`, `deleted_at = now()`, `scheduled_purge_at = now() + grace_days`
- revokes all sanctum tokens + sessions (you are logged out)
- sends `AccountDeletedNotification`
- fires the `AccountDeleted` event

**To restore:** simply log in again with the same credentials during the grace window. Auto-restore is silent.

**Errors:**
- 403 — self-service deletion disabled (`account.deletion.self_service=false`)
- 422 — wrong password
- 401 — unauthenticated

response.name: 422 — Wrong password

request.description:
**Not a new endpoint — just a normal login.** When `account.deletion.auto_restore_on_login=true` (default) and the credentials match a `status=deleted` user inside the grace window, the package:

1. Restores the user (`deleted_at = null`, `account_status = active`)
2. Drops the `deleted_accounts` row
3. Sends `AccountRestoredNotification`
4. Issues the session/token as if nothing happened

If the grace has expired, the login fails with the usual 401 invalid credentials response (the row's unique columns may have been nulled by the worker).

response.name: 200 — Restored and logged in
401 — Login blocked (disabled / suspended)

request.description:
Instagram-style self-pause. Flips the user to `deactivated`, revokes every session/token, sends `AccountDeactivatedNotification`.

**Distinct from delete:** no soft-delete, no purge worker, no countdown. The user simply hits a paused state and stays there until they choose to come back.

**Returning is automatic** — the next time the user logs in with valid credentials the package silently flips them to `active` and sends `AccountReactivatedNotification`. No separate reactivate endpoint, no time limit.

**Errors:**
- 403 — deactivation disabled (`account.deactivation.enabled=false`) or self-service disabled
- 422 — wrong password
- 401 — unauthenticated

response.name: 200 — Deactivated, log in to reactivate
422 — Wrong password

request.description:
**Not a new endpoint — just a normal login.** When `account.deactivation.auto_reactivate_on_login=true` (default) and the credentials match a `status=deactivated` user, the package:

1. Flips `account_status` back to `active`
2. Sends `AccountReactivatedNotification`
3. Fires `AccountStatusChanged`
4. Issues the session/token as if nothing happened

No time limit applies (unlike the delete grace flow). The user can come back days or years later.

item.name: Mobile — Bearer Token
item.description:
All requests configured for mobile apps and API clients.

**How it works:**
- Login sends `X-Client-Type: mobile` to receive a Bearer token even in `AUTH_MODE=both`.
- Every authenticated request includes `Authorization: Bearer {{auth_token}}`.
- No CSRF cookie or session cookie needed.
- A `refresh_token` is issued alongside the access token — use **Token Refresh** to rotate them before expiry.
- Step 3 of registration (Complete) returns the access + refresh tokens; both are auto-saved.

**Required:** `AUTH_MODE=api` or `AUTH_MODE=both`.

request.description:
**Step 1 of 3.** Initiates registration. Body is `{email}` only — **NO password** is sent at this step. Sends OTP + magic link to the email.

Does **not** create the user yet — that happens in step 3 after the password is set.

Auto-saves `temp_token` for Reverb channel subscription. After verifying the email (step 2) you will receive a `completion_token` to use in step 3.

**Extra fields example** (still no password):
```json
{
  "email": "joe@example.com",
  "phone": "+96170000000",
  "birthday": "1990-01-01"
}
```

**Errors:** 409 already registered · 422 validation · 429 rate limit

response.name: 201 — Verification email sent
409 — Email already registered
422 — Validation error

request.description:
**Step 2 of 3.** Returns a `completion_token` (UUID, 15 min TTL). Use it in step 3 to set your password and finalize the account.

**Errors:** 422 OTP invalid or expired

response.name: 201 — Email verified, completion_token issued
422 — Invalid OTP

request.description:
**Step 2 of 3 (alternative).** Returns a `completion_token` (UUID, 15 min TTL). Use it in step 3 to set your password and finalize the account.

Paste the full magic link URL from the verification email.

**Errors:** 422 invalid/expired or already used

response.name: 201 — Email verified, completion_token issued

request.description:
**Step 3 of 3.** Finalizes registration by setting the user's password. The `completion_token` is consumed (one-time use). On success the user is created, assigned the default role, logged in, and the `EmailVerified` event fires.

In Mobile mode the response includes `token` (access) and `refresh_token`. Both are auto-saved.

**Errors:** 422 completion_token invalid/expired · 422 password validation

response.name: 422 — completion_token invalid
422 — Password validation

request.description: Resends OTP/magic link for a pending registration. Always returns 200.

response.name: 200 — Sent (or pretended)

header.description: Optional — enables detailed device tracking in auth_sessions_extended

request.description:
Logs in and returns a Bearer `token` (access) + `refresh_token`. Both are auto-saved to collection variables.

`X-Client-Type: mobile` forces token mode in `AUTH_MODE=both`.

Enable `X-Device-Info` to track device model, platform, and OS version in the session record.

**Errors:** 401 wrong credentials/locked · 403 inactive/unverified · 422 validation · 429 rate limit
Returns the authenticated user's profile, roles, permissions, and active session count.
Revokes the current Bearer token and its associated session record.
Revokes ALL tokens and sessions for this user across every device.

Exchanges the current `refresh_token` for a new access token + refresh token pair. Both old tokens are revoked immediately.

Call this before the access token expires to keep the user logged in without re-entering credentials. The new tokens are auto-saved to `auth_token` and `refresh_token`.

Token lifetimes are configured per client type in `config/auth_system.php` under `token_ttl`.

**Errors:** 401 refresh token invalid, expired, or already used · 429 rate limit

response.name: 401 — Refresh token invalid

request.description: Sends OTP and/or magic link for password reset. Always returns 200 to prevent email enumeration.

response.name: 200 — Sent (or pretended)

request.description:
Step 1 of 2 for OTP-based password reset. Validates the OTP sent to the user's email and returns a short-lived reset_token (15 min). Use the reset_token in **2c. Confirm Reset** to set the new password.

**Errors:** 422 OTP invalid, 422 OTP expired, 422 validation

response.name: 200 — OTP verified, reset_token issued
422 — OTP expired
422 — Validation error (missing field)

request.description: Paste the full URL from the password reset email. Returns a `reset_token` valid for 15 minutes. Auto-saved to collection variables.

response.name: 200 — Magic link valid

exec: console.log('Logged in after reset. auth_token saved.');

request.description:
Final step of password reset. Accepts the reset_token from Step 2a or 2b and sets a new password.

- **logout_all: true** (default) — revoke all existing sessions/tokens before logging in
- **logout_all: false** — keep existing sessions, just update the password and log in

The user is automatically logged in after a successful reset. Response mirrors the login response: Bearer token for mobile/API clients, session cookie for web/SPA clients.

**Errors:** 422 reset_token invalid/expired, 422 validation

response.name: 200 — Reset + auto-login (logout_all: true)
200 — Reset + auto-login (logout_all: false, keep sessions)
422 — reset_token invalid or expired
422 — Validation error

request.description:
Changes password for the currently authenticated user.

**`logout_all: true`** — revokes all other tokens except the current one.

**Errors:** 422 current_password incorrect or validation failed

Returns ALL active sessions for the authenticated user — the current session and every other logged-in device — sorted by most recently active. Each entry includes platform, browser, OS, device info, IP, geo location, and an is_current flag marking which session belongs to this request.

The test script auto-saves a non-current session_id for use in the Revoke request below.

**Errors:** 401 unauthenticated

response.name: 200 — All sessions (current + others)
response.body:
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Sessions retrieved.",
  "data": {
    "sessions": [
      {
        "id": 3,
        "platform": "mobile",
        "browser": null,
        "os": "Android",
        "device_model": "SM-G991B",
        "device_marketing_name": "Samsung Galaxy S21",
        "device_code": "samsung_sm-g991b",
        "device_platform": "android",
        "ip_address": "10.0.0.5",
        "city": "Beirut",
        "country": "LB",
        "last_active_at": "2026-05-15T14:00:00.000000Z",
        "created_at": "2026-05-15T09:00:00.000000Z",
        "is_current": true
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "platform": "web",
        "browser": "Firefox",
        "os": "macOS",
        "device_model": null,
        "device_marketing_name": null,
        "device_code": null,
        "device_platform": null,
        "ip_address": "192.168.1.20",
        "city": "Paris",
        "country": "FR",
        "last_active_at": "2026-05-14T18:30:00.000000Z",
        "created_at": "2026-05-14T10:00:00.000000Z",

      },

request.description: Revokes the session with the given ID and its associated Sanctum token.

Lists all third-party API tokens owned by the authenticated user.

**Requires:** `AUTH_MODE=api` or `AUTH_MODE=both`.

Creates a new third-party API token. The `raw_token` is shown **only once** — store it securely.

**`abilities`:** array of strings. Use `["*"]` for full access.
**`expires_in_days`:** `null` = never expires. Max 3650.

Token format: `auth_at_{base64}`

Auto-saves `api_token_raw` and `api_token_id`.

response.name: 201 — Token created

request.description: Revokes the API token saved in `api_token_id`.

item.name: Use API Token on a Protected Route

request.description:
Example of calling a route protected by `auth.api-token` middleware. Replace the URL with your actual endpoint.

The middleware validates:
1. Token starts with `auth_at_`
2. SHA-256 hash matches a stored record
3. Token is active and not expired
4. Token has the required abilities

Returns the Google OAuth authorization URL. Open `data.redirect_url` in a browser to start the OAuth flow.

**Requires:** `AUTH_GOOGLE_ENABLED=true`

**Errors:** 403 Google OAuth disabled

exec: console.log('auth_token saved from Google OAuth:', body.data.token);

request.description:
Google redirects the user's browser here after authorization. In practice you do not call this directly.

**Handles 3 cases automatically:** existing provider link · email match · new user.

Auto-saves `auth_token` and `refresh_token`.

**Errors:** 403 disabled or inactive · 400 OAuth exchange failed

response.name: 202 — Requires link confirmation

item.name: Account (v2.4)
item.description: Self-service account deletion + auto-restore-on-login (mobile/Bearer flavor). See the Web/SPA copy of this folder for the full flow notes — the only difference is auth uses `Authorization: Bearer {{auth_token}}` instead of cookie + XSRF.

exec: // Token is revoked server-side — clear locally too.

request.description: Soft-deletes the authenticated mobile user. Same payload + side effects as the Web/SPA version. All sanctum tokens (including the current Bearer) are revoked server-side; the test script clears `auth_token` and `refresh_token` locally as well.

response.name: 422 — Wrong password

request.description: **Not a new endpoint — just the normal mobile login.** When the credentials match a `status=deleted` user inside the grace window the package silently restores the account, sends `AccountRestoredNotification`, and issues a fresh access + refresh token pair. The test script saves both for follow-up requests.

Mobile/Bearer flavor of Instagram-style self-pause. Same payload + side effects as the Web/SPA version. All sanctum tokens (including the current Bearer) are revoked server-side; the test script clears `auth_token` + `refresh_token` locally as well so the next request is unauthenticated until the user logs back in to reactivate.

response.name: 200 — Deactivated, log in to reactivate

request.description:
Same as the Web/SPA Reactivate-by-login but issues a Bearer token pair. The test script saves the new `auth_token` + `refresh_token` for follow-up requests.

No time limit applies — a deactivated user can come back at any point in the future.

item.name: API Token Client
item.description:
API tokens (format: auth_at_...) are long-lived, scoped keys for third-party integrations such as CI/CD pipelines, scripts, and external services. Completely separate from Sanctum session tokens.

Workflow:
  1. Login with Sanctum → get auth_token
  2. Create an API token → receive api_token_raw (shown ONCE only, save it)
  3. Use api_token_raw as Bearer on routes protected by auth.api-token middleware
  4. Revoke when done

Requires AUTH_API_TOKENS_ENABLED=true in .env

item.name: User — Manage Own Tokens
item.description: Authenticated user managing their own API tokens. Authenticates with Sanctum Bearer (auth_token).

request.description: Returns all API tokens owned by the authenticated user. Raw tokens are never returned — only metadata (id, name, abilities, expiry, last_used_at).

response.name: 200 — Token list

request.description:
Creates a new API token (auth_at_... format). The raw_token is shown ONLY once — save it immediately.

abilities: array of permission strings. Use ["*"] for full access.
expires_in_days: null for a non-expiring token.

response.name: 201 — Token created (with expiry)
201 — Token created (no expiry)
422 — Validation error

item.name: Use API Token on a Protected Route

request.description:
Uses the api_token_raw (auth_at_... format) as a Bearer token to authenticate against a route protected by auth.api-token middleware.

In your host app:
  ->middleware("auth.api-token")             // any valid token
  ->middleware("auth.api-token:read:deploy") // must have specific abilities

Replace /your-protected-route with a real route in your app.

response.name: 401 — Token invalid or expired

request.description: Revokes an API token by ID. Only the owner can revoke their own tokens. api_token_id is auto-saved by the Create request.

response.name: 404 — Token not found or not yours

item.name: Admin — Manage All Tokens
item.description: Admin-only endpoints. Requires super-admin or admin role.

request.description: Admin only — lists every API token across all users and system accounts. Requires super-admin or admin role.

response.name: 200 — All tokens
403 — Insufficient role

request.description:
Admin only — creates a system-level token not tied to any user. Useful for service-to-service authentication.

Use ["*"] for all abilities.

response.name: 201 — System token created
403 — Insufficient role

request.description: Admin only — updates abilities or expiry of any token. Omit a field to keep its current value. Set expires_in_days: null to make a token never expire.

response.name: 403 — Insufficient role

request.description: Admin only — revokes any token regardless of owner.

response.name: 403 — Insufficient role

item.name: Admin — Account Status (v2.4)
item.description:
Admin endpoints for reading and changing a user's `account_status` + the multi-admin **audit log** (status history + free-form notes).

**Auth:** standard sanctum Bearer (`auth_token`). The caller's role must match `config('auth_system.account.status.admin_ability')` (default `super-admin|admin`).

**Statuses:** `active`, `disabled`, `suspended`, `deleted`, `deactivated` (or whatever you add to `account.status.allowed`).

**Timed bans:** the update endpoint accepts an optional `expires_at` (ISO 8601 absolute) OR `duration_minutes` (relative integer). When the moment arrives the package auto-flips the user back to `active` through two paths: a lazy revert on the next status read (login, middleware) AND a scheduled `RevertExpiredAccountStatuses` sweep every `auto_unban.sweep_minutes` minutes (default 5). Omit both fields for a permanent ban.

**Which statuses can be timed:** controlled by `account.status.auto_unban.temporary_statuses` (default `['suspended']`). Statuses NOT in this list are permanent-only — sending an expiry alongside them returns 422. A null expiry on a temporary-capable status is still a permanent ban (forever).

**Side effects of a status change:**
- writes `account_status`, `status_changed_at`, `status_reason`, `status_expires_at`
- when leaving `active`, revokes all sanctum tokens + `auth_sessions_extended` rows for the user (toggle with `revoke_sessions_on_change`)
- when returning to `active`, clears `status_expires_at` (a pardon is a pardon)
- fires `AccountStatusChanged`
- optionally sends `AccountStatusChangedNotification` (off by default — toggle with `mail.account_notifications_enabled.status_changed`)

request.description:
Returns the user's current status plus when/why it last changed and the list of statuses your config allows. Useful for admin UIs.

**Errors:** 403 missing role; 404 user not found.

response.name: 403 — Missing admin role

request.description:
Marks the user as suspended. All of their sanctum tokens and tracked sessions are revoked, so they are logged out everywhere immediately. They will get a 401 with the localized `account_suspended` message on their next login attempt.

**`reason` vs `comment`:** keep `reason` short and tag-ish (it goes in `status_reason` and shows up in any quick listing). Put the longer multi-admin context — the "why", tickets, names — in `comment`. The audit log records both, so the next admin opening this user's history can understand the chain without pinging you.

response.name: 422 — Invalid status

request.description:
Timed ban via relative duration. `duration_minutes` is converted to an absolute `status_expires_at = now() + minutes`. When the moment arrives the package flips the user back to `active` automatically — two layers cooperate:

1. **Lazy revert** on the very next login or authenticated request that hits the user — they can log back in the instant their ban expires.
2. **Sweep worker** (`RevertExpiredAccountStatuses`) runs every `auto_unban.sweep_minutes` minutes (default 5) and reverts anything the lazy path missed.

Both paths fire `AccountStatusChanged` exactly once per user per revert.

Handy values:
- `60` → 1 hour
- `120` → 2 hours
- `1440` → 24 hours
- `10080` → 1 week
- `43200` → 30 days
- `525600` → 1 year

Timed ban via absolute ISO 8601 timestamp. Useful for an admin UI that lets the operator pick a date/time. If both `expires_at` and `duration_minutes` are sent, `expires_at` wins.

Validation: must parse as a date AND be in the future. Past timestamps return 422.

**Errors:** 422 expires_at is in the past or malformed.

response.name: 422 — expires_at in the past

item.name: Disable User with duration_minutes (rejected by default config)

request.description:
Demonstrates the `temporary_statuses` gate. Default config only marks `suspended` as timed-capable, so passing `duration_minutes` (or `expires_at`) alongside `status: disabled` returns 422.

To allow timed disables in your app:
```php
// config/auth_system.php
'account.status.auto_unban.temporary_statuses' => ['suspended', 'disabled'],
```

response.name: 422 — Status does not support timed bans

request.description: Marks the user as disabled. Same revocation side effects as Suspend; the only difference is the message key returned to the user on their next login attempt (`account_disabled` instead of `account_suspended`).

Flips the user back to `active`. They can log in normally again. Does not re-issue tokens — the user must log in from scratch.

This also **clears `status_expires_at`** unconditionally — a manual pardon overrides any pending auto-unban schedule.

Returns the **full audit history** for a user — every status transition (admin, user-initiated, automatic) plus any standalone admin notes — paginated, newest first.

Why: in a multi-admin team this is the answer to "why is this account disabled?" without pinging anyone. Each row shows who acted (admin id, the user themselves, or `system`), what changed, the `reason` (short tag) and `comment` (long admin note), the `source` context tag (`admin_endpoint`, `self_deactivate`, `auto_unban_lazy`, `login_auto_restore`, `purge_worker`, `admin_note`, etc.), `expires_at` if any, and the request IP/UA when applicable.

**Filters (query string):** `actor_type` (admin|user|system), `action` (status_change|note), `from` + `to` (ISO 8601), `page`, `per_page` (capped at `account.audit.history.max_per_page`, default 100).

**Errors:** 403 missing role; 404 audit or history endpoint disabled in config.

response.name: 200 — Paginated history
response.body:
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Audit history retrieved.",
  "data": {
    "items": [
      {
        "id": 87,
        "user_id": 42,
        "actor_type": "system",
        "actor_id": null,
        "action": "status_change",
        "from_status": "suspended",
        "to_status": "active",
        "reason": "Auto-unban: ban period elapsed.",
        "comment": null,
        "expires_at": null,
        "source": "auto_unban_sweep",
        "ip_address": null,
        "user_agent": null,
        "created_at": "2026-05-17T12:00:03.000000Z"
      },
      {
        "id": 86,
        "user_id": 42,
        "actor_type": "admin",
        "actor_id": 1,
        "action": "note",
        "from_status": null,

        "reason": null,
        "comment": "User emailed support apologising. Watching for repeat behavior.",
        "expires_at": null,
        "source": "admin_note",
        "ip_address": "10.0.0.5",
        "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 …",
        "created_at": "2026-05-17T11:30:00.000000Z"
      },
      {
        "id": 85,
        "user_id": 42,
        "actor_type": "admin",
        "actor_id": 1,
        "action": "status_change",
        "from_status": "active",
        "to_status": "suspended",

      }

    "total": 3,
    "last_page": 1

}

request.description:
Adds a free-form admin note about a user **without changing their status**. The note shows up in the user's audit history (`GET .../status/history`) and is visible to every admin with access.

Use this when you want the next admin to know something — "don't ban them, I'm waiting for product" — without pinging them in Slack.

**Fields:**
- `comment` (required, max 5000) — the note itself.
- `reason` (optional, max 1000) — short tag.

**Errors:** 403 missing role; 404 user not found OR notes endpoint disabled in config; 422 validation.

item.name: Reference
item.description: Response format examples and HTTP status code reference.

request.description:
Every error response follows this shape:
```json
{
  "success": false,
  "message": "Human readable error.",
  "errors": { }
}
```

**HTTP status codes used:**
- `200` OK
- `201` Created
- `400` Bad Request
- `401` Unauthorized
- `403` Forbidden
- `404` Not Found
- `409` Conflict (email already registered)
- `422` Unprocessable Entity (validation)
- `423` Locked (account lockout)
- `429` Too Many Requests (rate limit)

response.name: 422 — Validation envelope

item.name: v2.6 — Phone, 2FA & Trusted Devices
item.description:
v2.6 endpoints — phone capture + verification, two-factor authentication (TOTP / Email / SMS), backup codes, login challenge flow, password sudo mode, and trusted devices.

All requests in this folder assume Bearer Token auth (`{{auth_token}}`). For SPA/cookie mode add the `X-XSRF-TOKEN` header and remove `Authorization`.

**Trusted-device 2FA bypass (security model):** a device only skips the 2FA challenge when the login request carries BOTH `X-Browser-Fingerprint` (the device fingerprint) AND `X-Trusted-Device-Token` (a one-time secret issued when the device was trusted). Fingerprint alone never grants bypass. The token is returned once by `POST /auth/2fa/challenge` when `trust_device=true` and is auto-saved to `{{trusted_device_token}}`.

**Test variables added by these endpoints:**
- `challenge_token` — saved by Login when 2FA is enrolled
- `totp_secret` — saved by TOTP enrollment start (for use in QR/manual entry)
- `two_factor_method_id` — saved by 2FA methods list
- `trusted_device_id` — saved by Trusted Devices list
- `trusted_device_token` — saved by 2FA challenge when trust_device=true; send as X-Trusted-Device-Token on future logins

request.description:
Sends a one-time verification code to the supplied phone number via the configured channel (sms | voice | whatsapp). The default channel comes from `auth_system.phone.verification.default_channel`.

**Feature-gated:** returns 404 when `phone.enabled` is false.

**Errors:**
- 404 phone feature disabled
- 422 invalid phone format or driver misconfigured
- 429 rate limited

response.name: 422 — Invalid phone format
429 — Rate limited

request.description:
Verifies the OTP code sent to the phone. On success, stamps `phone_verified_at` on the authenticated user. Codes are single-use — even a valid code is rejected on the second attempt.

**Errors:**
- 422 phone_otp_invalid — wrong code (attempt counter bumps)
- 422 phone_otp_expired — past expiry window
- 422 phone_otp_locked — too many failed attempts
- 422 phone_otp_not_found — no active code for this phone

response.name: 422 — Too many attempts
422 — No active code

request.description: Lists the authenticated user's enrolled 2FA methods plus a summary of remaining backup codes. The first script auto-saves the first method id to `two_factor_method_id`.

response.name: 200 — Methods retrieved

request.description:
Generates a fresh TOTP secret + otpauth URI + inline SVG QR code. Saves the secret in the AuthTwoFactorMethod row as `verified_at = null`. The user scans the QR with Google Authenticator / Authy / 1Password / Bitwarden etc. and submits the first code via **Verify TOTP Enrollment** to complete enrollment.

If the user has no other verified 2FA method when verification succeeds, this is also the call where backup codes are generated (returned ONCE on the verify response).

Verifies the first TOTP code from the authenticator app. On success the method becomes active and (if this is the user's first verified 2FA method) backup codes are generated and returned ONCE in the response.

**Errors:**
- 422 two_factor_method_not_enrolled — call /start first
- 422 two_factor_code_invalid — wrong code

response.name: 200 — Enrolled (with backup codes on first method)

request.description: Sends a 6-digit code to the user's verified email address. Mask in the response is for UI display.
Verifies the code emailed during email 2FA enrollment.
Sends a 6-digit code to the user's verified phone via the configured SMS channel. Requires `phone_verified_at` to be set — otherwise returns 422.

response.name: 422 — No verified phone on file

request.description: Verifies the code received via SMS during SMS 2FA enrollment.
Marks the given method as the default one used at challenge time. Only one method can be the default at any time.

response.name: 422 — Method not found or unverified

request.description: Disables and deletes a 2FA method. If the user has no other enrolled methods after removal, the backup codes are deleted as well.
Returns metadata about the user's backup codes — total, used, remaining, last used. Never returns the plaintext codes (those are shown only once at generation time).
Wipes the existing backup code set and returns a fresh 8-code set. The plaintext codes are shown ONCE — store them on the client immediately.

When the authenticated user has ≥1 verified 2FA method AND the device is not trusted at the bypass level, login returns a `challenge_token` instead of a real Sanctum token. The client then calls **Verify Challenge** with the challenge_token + the 2FA code to obtain the real token.

**Trusted-device bypass (v2.6 security model):** to skip the 2FA challenge, the client must send BOTH the `X-Browser-Fingerprint` header AND the `X-Trusted-Device-Token` header obtained when the device was first trusted. Fingerprint alone is NOT enough — it is client-controlled and could be forged. When both match a non-revoked device at `>= bypass_2fa_min_level`, login issues a real token directly (no `requires_2fa`).

The test script auto-saves the `challenge_token` for the next request and persists the `auth_token` when no challenge is needed.

exec: console.log('auth_token saved (trusted-device bypass or no 2FA):', data.token);

request.description:
Completes the 2FA challenge. Pass the `code` from the user's TOTP app / email / SMS, the `method` they used, and `trust_device: true` to mark the device trusted so future logins skip 2FA.

**When `trust_device: true`**, the response includes a one-time `trusted_device_token`. STORE IT — the client must send it back as the `X-Trusted-Device-Token` header (alongside `X-Browser-Fingerprint`) on future logins to bypass 2FA. It is returned exactly once and cannot be recovered. The test script auto-saves it to the `trusted_device_token` collection variable.

If the code is wrong, the challenge's `attempts` counter increments — after 5 failures the challenge is invalidated and the user must log in again.

If the supplied code is a backup code, set `method: "backup"` (or omit it — backup codes are tried as fallback automatically).

response.name: 401 — Too many failed attempts (challenge invalidated)

exec: console.log('trusted_device_token saved — send as X-Trusted-Device-Token on future logins');

request.description: User chose a different enrolled method (e.g. "my phone is dead, send to email instead"). Issues a fresh code on the new method and re-uses the same challenge_token.

response.name: 200 — Switched, new code sent

request.description: Re-sends the code for the currently-selected method (no-op for TOTP).

GitHub-style sudo mode. When `auth.2fa` middleware is set to `password_confirm` mode and the user has no 2FA enrolled, calling this endpoint grants a short-lived (15 min by default) step-up window. Used as a fallback for `Require2FA` middleware.

**Errors:**
- 422 wrong password

Returns every trusted device for the authenticated user with its effective trust level (derived from the configured `level_assignment` mode) and an `is_current` flag for the device making this request. Saves the first non-current trusted device id to `trusted_device_id`.

response.name: 200 — Devices listed
response.body:
{
    "success": true,
    "message": "Trusted devices retrieved.",
    "data": {
        "devices": [
            {
                "id": 21,
                "device_name": "iPhone 15 Pro",
                "platform": "mobile",
                "browser": null,
                "os": "iOS",
                "level": "high",
                "stored_level": "high",
                "admin_granted": false,
                "trusted_at": "2025-12-10T08:00:00.000000Z",
                "last_seen_at": "2026-05-23T11:50:00.000000Z",
                "is_current": true
            },
            {
                "id": 22,
                "device_name": "MacBook Pro — Chrome",
                "platform": "web",
                "browser": "Chrome",
                "os": "macOS",
                "level": "medium",
                "stored_level": "low",
                "admin_granted": false,
                "trusted_at": "2026-03-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
                "last_seen_at": "2026-05-22T18:00:00.000000Z",
                "is_current": false
            },
            {
                "id": 23,
                "device_name": "Galaxy S21 — Chrome",
                "platform": "mobile",
                "browser": "Chrome",
                "os": "Android",
                "level": "low",
                "stored_level": "low",
                "admin_granted": false,
                "trusted_at": "2026-05-05T11:00:00.000000Z",
                "last_seen_at": "2026-05-20T10:00:00.000000Z",
                "is_current": false
            }
        ]
    }
}

request.description:
Revokes a single trusted device. The revocation matrix applies:
- `low` cannot revoke anything
- `medium` can revoke `low` only
- `high` can revoke `low` + `medium` + `high`
- A device can always revoke itself (if trusted)

Password is required for step-up. The route is also wrapped in `auth.2fa` middleware so users with 2FA enrolled must additionally complete a fresh challenge first.

**Errors:**
- 403 actor's level cannot revoke target's level
- 422 password missing or wrong
- 404 trusted device not found

response.name: 403 — Trust level too low to revoke this device

request.description:
Nuclear option — revokes EVERY trusted device the user has, including the device making this request. Available to any trusted device (low/medium/high). Requires password confirmation; if the user has 2FA enrolled the `auth.2fa` middleware requires a fresh 2FA challenge too.

Use this after suspected account compromise.

response.name: 403 — Untrusted device cannot revoke

item.name: Social Profile Completion
item.description: When `social.profile_completion.enabled` is true, a brand-new Google user who is missing the host's required registration fields is NOT created or logged in by the OAuth callback. Instead the callback returns `requires_profile_completion` + a `completion_token`; the frontend collects the required fields and submits them here. Enforces the same `extra_fields_rules` + phone rules as the email flow. No user row exists until completion.

request.description: The OAuth callback is a browser redirect from Google — you normally don't call it directly from Postman. This item documents the RESPONSE shape your frontend must handle when profile completion is enabled and the user is brand new. Save the `completion_token` and show an onboarding form.

response.name: 202 — Profile completion required
200 — Logged in (existing user or completion disabled)

request.description:
Submits the host's required registration fields to finish a brand-new social signup. Validates against the same `extra_fields_rules` + phone rules as the email flow. On success: creates the user (email already verified by Google), links the social account, and issues the real token. The test script saves `auth_token`.

**Errors:**
- 422 validation — a required field is missing/invalid
- 422 completion_token_invalid — token expired or already used
- 404 — `social.profile_completion.enabled` is false

response.name: 422 — Required field missing
422 — Completion token invalid or expired
404 — Profile completion disabled

item.name: Require2FA Middleware — Sample Behaviors
item.description: These items document the THREE possible responses from any endpoint protected by `auth.2fa` middleware when the session has not been 2FA-verified recently. The actual URL depends on where the host app attaches the middleware (your sensitive endpoints). Use these as a reference for what your client must handle.

item.name: Protected endpoint (user has 2FA enrolled — step-up required)

request.description: When the user has ≥1 verified 2FA method and the current session has no recent 2FA stamp, the middleware issues a fresh challenge_token and returns 403. The client must complete `/auth/2fa/challenge` with this token, then retry the original request.
When `auth_system.two_factor.middleware_behavior=block` and the user has no 2FA enrolled, the middleware hard-rejects the request.

Default behavior. When the user has no 2FA enrolled, the middleware asks the client to confirm the password via `/auth/password/confirm`. Once confirmed, retries within `sudo_ttl_minutes` (default 15) pass through.
